tools.name: query
tools.description:
Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

<example description="Emails from specific mailboxes">
  {
    "in": [{"/" : "dxn:echo:@:YYYYYY"}, {"/" : "dxn:echo:@:XXXXXXX"}],
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

includeContent.description: Include the full object data in the response.

limit.description: The maximum number of results to return.

includeQueues.description: Search in queues as well as spaces. Only use this if searching for emails.

tools.name: query
tools.description:
Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

<output_format>

  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.

    "text": "cyberdyne bob",

</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3

    "limit": 20
  }

# DXOS — The Decentralized Operating System

## Overview

<cite index="1-3">DXOS is an open source framework for building real-time, collaborative web applications that run entirely on the client and communicate peer-to-peer, without the need for centralized servers.</cite> The name stands for **Decentralized Operating System**. <cite index="9-3">DXOS is an independent public organization whose mission is to provide open source technologies that allow for a free and open decentralized internet.</cite>

## Company Details

- **Founded:** <cite index="2-5">DXOS was founded in 2021.</cite>
- **Headquarters:** <cite index="2-6">DXOS is headquartered in New York, NY.</cite>
- **Status:** <cite index="2-8">DXOS is a Private company.</cite>
- **Industry:** <cite index="2-7">DXOS's primary industry is Software Development Applications.</cite>
- **Investors:** <cite index="2-10">Backed VC, BlueYard Capital, Empty Set Group, and PL Capital (United States) have invested in DXOS.</cite>
- **License:** Open-source under the MIT license.
- **Related Entity:** <cite index="28-3,28-4">Braneframe is a Web3 company building peer-to-peer privacy-preserving applications built on open-source protocols. It is a leading project in the DXOS (Decentralized Operating System) ecosystem, whose participants are working toward an alternative to the tech giant-dominated public cloud.</cite>

## Mission & Philosophy

<cite index="2-1">DXOS is a developer of a decentralized platform designed to create the first functionally and commercially competitive, fully decentralized alternative to the corporate cloud.</cite> <cite index="2-4">The company offers a network operating system, infrastructure platform, programming framework, and toolchain, enabling businesses to develop privacy-preserving applications for its users and a fairer marketplace for open-source developers.</cite>

<cite index="1-21,1-22">They're setting a new standard for how modern software is built by reducing reliance on centralized servers and focusing on scalable peer-to-peer data networks. Their framework empowers anyone to create real-time collaborative tools within a privacy-focused, local-first workspace that is enhanced by AI capabilities.</cite>

## Core Technology Stack

The DXOS SDK includes three primary protocols/components:

### 1. ECHO (Data Layer)
<cite index="1-6">ECHO is an open source database architecture that incorporates transparent data replication and conflict resolution for secure and scalable local storage.</cite> <cite index="12-8">It serves as a peer-to-peer database and reactive state container for offline-first, real-time, collaborative apps.</cite>

### 2. HALO (Identity Layer)
<cite index="1-7">HALO seamlessly manages digital identities using public/private key pairs allowing nuanced control over access to applications, databases, and networked devices.</cite> <cite index="4-5">HALO is a decentralized identity and access control protocol that makes it easier to build collaborative applications.</cite>

### 3. MESH (Network Layer)
<cite index="1-9">MESH extends existing internet protocols to enable secure and resilient peer-to-peer networks without the need for centralized server infrastructure.</cite>

### CRDT & Automerge
<cite index="1-11,1-12">DXOS leverages Automerge — a powerful CRDT that enables multiple concurrent users to operate on the same data without loss or conflict.</cite>

## Architecture & How It Works

<cite index="1-14,1-15">The only reason you need a central server is to serve your application shell. Everything else happens in the client and works peer-to-peer.</cite>

- <cite index="1-17">User identity, data storage, synchronization and networking are all handled natively using the DXOS client API.</cite>
- <cite index="1-19">Data is automatically synchronized across networked devices using WebRTC for cloudless peer-to-peer networking.</cite>
- <cite index="4-6">There are still signaling servers for peer discovery, but all data exchange happens peer-to-peer, and no servers have access to the data.</cite>
- <cite index="20-11">The platform wraps its three components together in a set of SDKs (currently, React and TypeScript) that enable developers to deliver sophisticated applications quickly.</cite>

## Flagship Application: Composer

<cite index="12-4">To show off the power of DXOS, they've built Composer: a local-first workspace that is designed for small team collaboration.</cite>

<cite index="31-24">Composer blends the power of popular file formats (docs, sheets, sketch & tables) while allowing you to build plugins, deploy custom functions and leverage modern LLMs.</cite>

Key Composer features include:
- <cite index="31-25">Realtime collaboration inside of a modern markdown editor.</cite>
- <cite index="31-27">Threaded comments across multiple document formats.</cite>
- <cite index="31-29">Custom plugins to enhance functionality.</cite>
- <cite index="31-31">Custom schemas to work with structured data.</cite>
- <cite index="31-33">A local-first spreadsheet that supports custom functions.</cite>
- <cite index="31-6">Machine learning to build agentic workflows that enhance team productivity while protecting intellectual property.</cite>
- <cite index="31-13">Custom agents that use Composer data as a dynamic context window.</cite>

<cite index="40-1,40-2">Composer is built with extensibility at its core. Everything within the application is built using the same App Framework APIs, from the UI down to data and business logic; anything in Composer can be customized and enhanced.</cite>

### Privacy in Composer
<cite index="15-7,15-8">Composer is built on the peer-to-peer protocols of DXOS which are designed to be private by default. The data is stored on your device and only shared with others you explicitly invite.</cite>

## DXOS Agents

<cite index="38-3,38-4,38-5,38-6">The DXOS Agent provides offline backup and synchronization capabilities. It continuously replicates all data from all browser sessions. When browser sessions are offline, it will replicate any changes from spaces to any peers in the space. This allows changes to a space to be available even if the other peer was offline when the change was made.</cite>

<cite index="32-1,32-2">Agents are DXOS instances running without an attached user interface. A common use of agents is to run on a server and provide availability if all other devices are offline, but this is completely optional.</cite>

<cite index="4-2">DXOS is launching a fully-managed service to host agents.</cite> <cite index="33-1,33-2">Their vision is to enable significant savings through the interoperability of DXOS applications. Local-first apps can share the same agent and data storage, maintaining data privacy without extra charges for each additional app.</cite>

## Community & Development

- <cite index="3-2">DXOS has 64 repositories available on GitHub.</cite>
- <cite index="6-4">The main repository is a TypeScript implementation of the DXOS protocols, SDK, toolchain and Composer.</cite>
- <cite index="7-6">DXOS was created by a small core team of developers from many countries.</cite>
- <cite index="1-23">The DXOS Community is made of a diverse group of open source developers from all over the world.</cite>
- <cite index="9-4,9-5">DXOS.org is a decentralized global community of open source developers. The community is organized via an independent foundation (DXOS.foundation), which is funded by members of the community.</cite>

## Key Differentiators

1. **Local-first:** Applications work offline and store data locally on user devices.
2. **Peer-to-peer:** No centralized servers required for data exchange; uses WebRTC.
3. **Privacy-preserving:** Data never passes through third-party servers.
4. **Real-time collaboration:** Powered by CRDTs (Automerge) for conflict-free concurrent editing.
5. **Fully open source:** MIT licensed, with transparent development.
6. **Extensible:** Plugin architecture (especially in Composer) allows deep customization.
7. **AI-enhanced:** Support for local AI models and agentic workflows.

## Links

- **Website:** https://dxos.org
- **Documentation:** https://docs.dxos.org
- **GitHub:** https://github.com/dxos
- **Blog:** https://blog.dxos.org
- **Composer:** https://dxos.org/composer

tools.name: query
tools.description:
Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

<example description="Emails from specific mailboxes">
  {
    "in": [{"/" : "dxn:echo:@:YYYYYY"}, {"/" : "dxn:echo:@:XXXXXXX"}],
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

## DXOS — Research Summary

### Overview
DXOS is the Decentralized Operating System — a platform for building fully decentralized applications.
DXOS is an open source framework for building real-time, collaborative web applications that run entirely on the client and communicate peer-to-peer, without the need for centralized servers.

The project is setting a new standard for how modern software is built by reducing reliance on centralized servers and focusing on scalable peer-to-peer data networks. The framework empowers anyone to create real-time collaborative tools within a privacy-focused, local-first workspace that is enhanced by AI capabilities.

### Company Details

-
DXOS was founded in 2021.
DXOS is headquartered in New York, NY.
The company has 11-50 employees.
DXOS is a Private company.
DXOS was created by a small core team of developers from many countries.
Backed VC, BlueYard Capital, Empty Set Group, and PL Capital (United States) have invested in DXOS.
The project is open-source under the MIT license.

### Core Technology Stack

The DXOS SDK consists of three core components that work together:

1. **ECHO** —
ECHO is an open source database architecture that incorporates transparent data replication and conflict resolution for secure and scalable local storage.
It serves as a peer-to-peer database and reactive state container for offline-first, real-time, collaborative apps.
HALO seamlessly manages digital identities using public/private key pairs allowing nuanced control over access to applications, databases, and networked devices.
MESH extends existing internet protocols to enable secure and resilient peer-to-peer networks without the need for centralized server infrastructure.
These three components are wrapped together in a set of SDKs (currently, React and TypeScript) that enable developers to deliver sophisticated applications quickly.
### How It Works
The only reason you need a central server is to serve your application shell. Everything else happens in the client and works peer-to-peer.
User identity, data storage, synchronization and networking are all handled natively using the DXOS client API.
Data is automatically synchronized across networked devices using WebRTC for cloudless peer-to-peer networking.
DXOS leverages Automerge — a powerful CRDT that enables multiple concurrent users to operate on the same data without loss or conflict.
There are still signaling servers for peer discovery, but all data exchange happens peer-to-peer, and no servers have access to the data.
### Flagship Application: Composer
To show off the power of DXOS, they've built Composer: a local-first workspace that is designed for small team collaboration.
Composer is a collaborative productivity app where developers can build and organize knowledge, extend with custom data and UI, and run private AI against their knowledge locally.
Composer is an open source "Super App" that provides teams with a fully customizable and extensible experience in a single integrated environment.

Key features include:

-
Blending the power of popular file formats (docs, sheets, sketch & tables) inside of a collaborative local-first workspace.
Everything inside of Composer is a plugin. This means you can leverage core functionality or build out whatever you need.
Leveraging machine learning to build agentic workflows that enhance team productivity while protecting intellectual property.
Import and export your entire database as plain, human readable markdown, JSON, and media files.
Composer is not yet ready for production. There are bugs, and breaking changes may occur.
### DXOS Agents
DXOS is a decentralized and privacy-preserving platform which communicates peer-to-peer. When all the peers go offline, it's impossible for any new peers to replicate. This is where agents come in.
Agents are just like any other peer, but are assumed to have more uptime on the network. They should typically be running on servers or desktop machines which are always plugged in.
DXOS is launching a fully-managed service to host agents.

### Key Differentiators (DXOS vs. Traditional SaaS)

- **Local-first**: Data is stored locally on the user's device and synchronized peer-to-peer.
- **Privacy by default**:
Composer is built on the peer-to-peer protocols of DXOS which are designed to be private by default. The data is stored on your device and only shared with others you explicitly invite.
- **Offline support**:
Supports offline functionality with automatic synchronization upon reconnection.
- **No vendor lock-in**:
You own everything inside of Composer. No spyware, no algorithms, no lock-in.
- **Extensible**:
Build modular applications based around plugins & capabilities, such as Composer.

### Community & Development

-
DXOS has 64 repositories available on GitHub.
The community spans 8 cities worldwide, has 30+ contributors, and 200+ community members.
The DXOS community is made of a diverse group of open source developers from all over the world.
### Current Status

The DXOS SDK is under active development, with the current focus being building features and functionality that enables the development of Composer. To this end, they're currently only able to provide limited support to developers using the SDK, and will prioritize suggestions and contributions that align with their work on Composer.

### Links

- **Website**: https://dxos.org
- **Documentation**: https://docs.dxos.org
- **GitHub**: https://github.com/dxos/dxos
- **Blog**: https://blog.dxos.org
- **Composer**: https://dxos.org/composer

## DXOS — Research Summary

### Overview

DXOS (Decentralized Operating System) is an open-source platform for building fully decentralized, real-time, collaborative web applications that run entirely on the client and communicate peer-to-peer, without the need for centralized servers. The project aims to set a new standard for modern software by reducing reliance on centralized servers and focusing on scalable peer-to-peer data networks, empowering developers to create real-time collaborative tools within a privacy-focused, local-first workspace enhanced by AI capabilities.

### Company Details

- **Founded**: 2021
- **Headquarters**: New York, NY
- **Company Size**: 11–50 employees
- **Status**: Private company
- **Team**: Created by a small core team of developers from many countries
- **Investors**: Backed VC, BlueYard Capital, Empty Set Group, and PL Capital (United States)
- **License**: Open-source under the MIT license

### Core Technology Stack

The DXOS SDK consists of three core components:

1. **ECHO** — An open-source database architecture that incorporates transparent data replication and conflict resolution for secure and scalable local storage. It serves as a peer-to-peer database and reactive state container for offline-first, real-time, collaborative apps.

2. **HALO** — Seamlessly manages digital identities using public/private key pairs allowing nuanced control over access to applications, databases, and networked devices.

3. **MESH** — Extends existing internet protocols to enable secure and resilient peer-to-peer networks without the need for centralized server infrastructure.

These three components are wrapped together in a set of SDKs (currently React and TypeScript) that enable developers to deliver sophisticated applications quickly.

### How It Works

- The only reason you need a central server is to serve your application shell; everything else happens in the client and works peer-to-peer.
- User identity, data storage, synchronization and networking are all handled natively using the DXOS client API.
- Data is automatically synchronized across networked devices using WebRTC for cloudless peer-to-peer networking.
- DXOS leverages Automerge, a powerful CRDT (Conflict-free Replicated Data Type) that enables multiple concurrent users to operate on the same data without loss or conflict.
- There are still signaling servers for peer discovery, but all data exchange happens peer-to-peer, and no servers have access to the data.

### Flagship Application: Composer

Composer is a local-first workspace designed for small team collaboration. It is a collaborative productivity app where developers can build and organize knowledge, extend with custom data and UI, and run private AI against their knowledge locally. Key features include:

- Blending popular file formats (docs, sheets, sketch & tables) inside a collaborative local-first workspace
- Everything inside Composer is a plugin, enabling full customization
- Machine learning-powered agentic workflows that enhance team productivity while protecting intellectual property
- Import and export entire databases as plain, human-readable markdown, JSON, and media files
- Real-time collaboration, threaded comments, custom schemas, spreadsheets, and diagrams

Note: Composer is not yet ready for production; there are bugs and breaking changes may occur.

### DXOS Agents

DXOS agents address the challenge of peer availability: when all peers go offline, no new peers can replicate data. Agents are peers assumed to have more uptime, typically running on servers or always-on machines. DXOS offers both an open-source CLI tool for self-hosting agents and a fully-managed service for convenient hosting, backup, and uptime.

### Key Differentiators vs. Traditional SaaS

- **Local-first**: Data stored locally on the user's device, synced peer-to-peer
- **Privacy by default**: Built on peer-to-peer protocols, data only shared with explicitly invited parties
- **Offline support**: Works offline with automatic sync upon reconnection
- **No vendor lock-in**: Users own everything; no spyware, algorithms, or lock-in
- **Extensible**: Modular plugin-based architecture for custom applications

### Community & Development

- 64 repositories on GitHub
- 8 cities worldwide, 30+ contributors, 200+ community members
- Active community of diverse open-source developers from around the world

### Current Status

The DXOS SDK is under active development, with the primary focus on building features and functionality for Composer. Limited support is available for external SDK developers, with priority given to contributions aligned with Composer development.
